events.title: Pytheas writes of Thule
events.description: Greek geographer Pytheas describes a northern land called Thule. Some scholars suggest this is Iceland, though his account of milk, honey and fruit makes Norway or the Faroes more likely.

events.title: Roman coins reach Iceland
events.description: Roman currency dating to the third century has been found in Iceland. It is unknown whether the coins were brought at that time or arrived later via Vikings.

events.title: Longhouse at Stöðvarfjörður
events.description: Archaeologists uncovered a longhouse at Stöðvarfjörður dated to as early as 800, predating the traditional 874 settlement date and suggesting an earlier human presence.

events.title: Irish papar in Iceland
events.description: The Landnamabok records Irish monks - the papar - living in Iceland before the Norse, leaving behind bells, books and crosiers. A cabin at Hafnir on Reykjanes was abandoned between 770 and 880.

events.title: Naddodd discovers Snaeland
events.description: Norse sailor Naddodd, blown off course on a voyage from Norway to the Faroes, drifts to the east coast of Iceland and names the country Snaeland - Snowland.

events.title: Gardar Svavarsson circumnavigates
events.description: Swedish sailor Gardar Svavarsson accidentally drifts to Iceland, circumnavigates it confirming it is an island, and winters at Husavik. He names it Gardarsholmi.

events.title: Hrafna-Floki first deliberate voyage
events.description: Hrafna-Floki Vilgerdarson becomes the first Norseman to sail to Iceland intentionally. After a brutal winter at Bardastrond followed by a green summer, he returns to Norway with news of the habitable land.

events.title: Ingolfur Arnarson settles Reykjavik
events.description: Norwegian chieftain Ingolfur Arnarson and his wife Hallveig Frodadottir found Iceland's first permanent settlement at Reykjavik - Smoke Cove - so named for the geothermal steam.

events.title: Norse mass migration begins
events.description: Norwegian chieftains, families, slaves and Hiberno-Norse settlers from the British Isles arrive in waves, many fleeing the unification of Norway under Harald Fairhair or seeking new farmland.

events.title: All arable land claimed
events.description: Within roughly 60 years the entire habitable coast and lowlands are claimed by settlers. Ari Thorgilsson's Islendingabok records that Iceland was fully settled within this period.

events.title: Althing established at Thingvellir
events.description: The ruling chieftains establish the Althing, a national assembly meeting each summer at Thingvellir. With no central executive, the chieftains amend laws, settle disputes and appoint juries. It is among the world's oldest parliaments.

events.title: Eldgja eruption
events.description: The Eldgja fissure in southern Iceland erupts violently, spreading sulfuric haze as far as northern China and causing food crises across Europe. Many Icelanders interpret it as divine wrath, accelerating conversion to Christianity.

events.title: Erik the Red settles Greenland
events.description: Outlawed from Iceland, Erik the Red leads settlers west to found a Norse colony in Greenland. The expedition extends the Icelandic sphere into the North Atlantic.

events.title: Iceland converts to Christianity
events.description: Facing civil war between pagans and Christians, the Althing appoints chieftain Thorgeir Ljosvetningagodi to arbitrate. He rules that Iceland will convert to Christianity but pagans may worship privately.

events.title: Leif Erikson reaches Vinland
events.description: Sailing from Greenland with an Icelandic crew, Leif Erikson reaches North America, establishing a brief Norse presence at L'Anse aux Meadows in modern Newfoundland.

events.title: First Icelandic bishop consecrated
events.description: Isleifur Gissurarson is consecrated as the first Icelandic bishop by Adalbert of Hamburg, formalising the Church's organisational presence in Iceland.

events.title: Islendingabok written
events.description: Scholar Ari Thorgilsson the Wise writes Islendingabok - the Book of Icelanders - one of the earliest and most reliable accounts of the settlement and early Commonwealth period.

events.title: Sagas of Icelanders written
events.description: Icelanders begin documenting the nation's history and feuds in the prose sagas - among the great literary achievements of medieval Europe and a primary source for Norse history.

events.title: Age of the Sturlungs begins
events.description: Power concentrates in a handful of clans. The Sturlungar family, including Snorri Sturluson and his nephew Sturla Sighvatsson, wages decades of internal war that exhausts the Commonwealth.

events.title: Snorri Sturluson swears to Haakon IV
events.description: Saga author and chieftain Snorri Sturluson becomes a vassal of Norwegian king Haakon IV, beginning Norway's slow consolidation of power over Iceland.

events.title: Old Covenant signed
events.description: After decades of internal war, Icelandic chieftains sign the Gamli sattmali, accepting the sovereignty of the Norwegian crown. The Icelandic Commonwealth ends.

events.title: Jonsbok law code adopted
events.description: The Norwegian-derived Jonsbok law code replaces the older Commonwealth law, completing the legal transition under the Norwegian crown.

events.title: Stadamal disputes settled
events.description: The second stadamal dispute over tithes from proprietary churches concludes in favour of the Church, shifting wealth and power from secular chieftains to ecclesiastical authorities.

events.title: Little Ice Age begins to bite
events.description: Shorter growing seasons and colder winters reduce barley yields and force increased grain imports. Marginal Icelandic farmland becomes harder to work, beginning centuries of hardship.

events.title: Vistarband peasant binding emerges
events.description: A serfdom-like institution called vistarband develops, binding peasants to landowners for one year at a time and limiting rural mobility for centuries.

events.title: Stockfish trade rises
events.description: Church fast days drive Continental European demand for dried cod. Stockfish becomes the dominant Icelandic export and remains so for centuries.

events.title: Norwegian royal line ends
events.description: The death of Olaf II of Denmark extinguishes the Norwegian male royal line. Norway and Iceland pass into the same crown as Denmark, ending direct Norwegian rule.

events.title: Kalmar Union formed
events.description: Denmark, Sweden and Norway unite in the Kalmar Union under Danish leadership. Iceland's trade orientation begins shifting decisively toward Copenhagen.

events.title: English Age begins
events.description: English merchants and fishing fleets dominate Iceland's external trade, drawn by abundant cod. The Icelandic trade accounts for over 10 percent of the port of Hull's total commerce.

events.title: Greenland Norse colony dies out
events.description: Iceland's sister Norse colony in Greenland, founded by Erik the Red, dies out completely. The reasons - climate, isolation, conflict with Inuit - remain debated.

events.title: German Age in trade
events.description: Hanseatic German merchants displace the English in Iceland's external trade. Germans own fishing boats and rent them to Icelanders, exporting the catch to the Continent.

events.title: Kalmar Union dissolves
events.description: Sweden breaks from the Kalmar Union. Iceland, along with Norway, Faroe and Greenland, remains tied to the Danish crown.

events.title: Bishop Ogmundur deported
events.description: Danish officials deport Ogmundur Palsson, Catholic bishop of Skalholt, as Christian III of Denmark begins forcing the Lutheran Reformation on his subjects.

events.title: Last Catholic bishop beheaded
events.description: Jon Arason, Catholic bishop of Holar, and his two sons are beheaded at Skalholt after defeat at the Battle of Saudafell. Iceland is forcibly Lutheranised and remains so to this day.

events.title: Danish trade monopoly imposed
events.description: Pursuing mercantilist policies, Denmark forbids Iceland from trading with any country other than Denmark. The Danish-Icelandic Trade Monopoly will remain in force for 184 years.

events.title: Turkish Abductions
events.description: A Barbary pirate fleet raids the coasts of Iceland and abducts nearly 300 Icelanders into North African slavery. Denmark offers little defence; the trauma marks Icelandic memory for centuries.

events.title: Dutch and French traders rise
events.description: Despite the monopoly, an illicit trade with Dutch and French merchants flourishes, partly compensating for the inefficiencies of forced Danish channels.

events.title: Absolute monarchy in Denmark-Norway
events.description: Frederick III of Denmark introduces absolute monarchy. Icelanders relinquish their remaining autonomy to the Crown, including the right to consent to legislation.

events.title: Laki eruption
events.description: The Laki fissure erupts violently, spitting out 12.5 cubic kilometres of lava and releasing toxic ash and gas across the country. It is one of the largest effusive eruptions in recorded history.

events.title: Moduhardindin - the Mist Hardships
events.description: Laki's aftermath kills 9,000 people directly and 80 percent of livestock. The ensuing famine kills roughly a quarter of Iceland's population. Climatic conditions reach their lowest point since settlement.

events.title: Trade monopoly relaxed
events.description: After 184 years, the Danish-Icelandic Trade Monopoly is formally ended. Trade is opened to all Danish subjects, though foreign merchants remain restricted.

events.title: Althing abolished
events.description: The old Althing, by then a purely judicial body, is abolished after roughly 870 years of continuous existence. The institutional link to the Commonwealth is severed.

events.title: Jorgensen's brief reign
events.description: Danish adventurer Jorgen Jorgensen arrives, declares Iceland independent and pronounces himself ruler. Two months later HMS Talbot restores Danish rule and arrests him.

events.title: Treaty of Kiel
events.description: Following the Napoleonic Wars, the Treaty of Kiel separates the kingdoms of Denmark and Norway. Denmark keeps Iceland, Faroe and Greenland as dependencies.

events.title: Fjolnismenn lead national revival
events.description: A group of Danish-educated Icelandic intellectuals, the Fjolnismenn, lead a romantic-nationalist cultural revival inspired by Continental European movements.

events.title: Althing restored
events.description: Denmark agrees to re-establish the Althing as a consultative assembly. Independence advocates led by Jon Sigurdsson claim continuity with the medieval Commonwealth Althing.

events.title: Constitution and home rule granted
events.description: A thousand years after the first settlement, Denmark grants Iceland a constitution and limited home rule over domestic affairs. The millennial anniversary becomes a national rallying point.

events.title: Mass emigration to Manitoba
events.description: Worsening climate and limited opportunity drive thousands of Icelanders to emigrate, mostly to Manitoba in Canada, creating a lasting Western Icelandic diaspora.

events.title: Hannes Hafstein - first Minister for Iceland
events.description: Home rule is expanded. Hannes Hafstein becomes the first Minister for Iceland in the Danish cabinet, the first Icelander to hold such a post.

events.title: World War I isolates Iceland
events.description: Iceland is neutral but becomes more isolated. Living standards fall, the treasury becomes heavily indebted, and food shortages stoke famine fears. Trade with the UK rises sharply.

events.title: Act of Union - Kingdom of Iceland
events.description: Denmark and Iceland sign the Danish-Icelandic Act of Union, recognising Iceland as a fully sovereign kingdom in personal union with the Danish king. Iceland gains its own flag and permanent neutrality.

events.title: Great Depression hits exports
events.description: The Wall Street Crash sends Iceland's export economy into freefall. Total exports collapse from 74 million kronur in 1929 to 48 million in 1932 and do not recover until 1939.

events.title: Spanish Civil War halves saltfish exports
events.description: The outbreak of the Spanish Civil War cuts Iceland's saltfish exports by half, deepening the depression. Government interference in the economy expands sharply.

events.title: All-party government formed
events.description: With Germany occupying Czechoslovakia and war looming, Iceland forms an all-party government. Lufthansa's request for landing rights is refused.

events.title: Germany occupies Denmark
events.description: Operation Weserubung severs communications between Iceland and Denmark. The Danish king cannot exercise the personal union; Iceland is effectively cut adrift.

events.title: Iceland takes own foreign affairs
events.description: Parliament assumes temporary control of foreign affairs and the Coast Guard, and elects Sveinn Bjornsson as provisional governor. Iceland becomes de facto fully sovereign.

events.title: British invade in Operation Fork
events.description: British forces sail into Reykjavik harbour and occupy Iceland to forestall a German move. There is no resistance. The government protests the breach of neutrality but treats the troops as guests.

events.title: US takes over occupation
events.description: Under a US-Icelandic agreement that recognises Iceland's absolute independence, the United States assumes responsibility for defence. Up to 40,000 American troops are stationed - outnumbering all adult Icelandic men.

events.title: Act of Union expires
events.description: The 25-year Act of Union agreement with Denmark expires. With Denmark still under Nazi occupation, Iceland prepares to formally sever ties.

events.title: Independence plebiscite
events.description: Icelanders vote in a four-day plebiscite: 97 percent in favour of ending the union with the Danish king, 95 percent in favour of the new republican constitution.

events.title: Republic of Iceland declared
events.description: Iceland becomes an independent republic with Sveinn Bjornsson as first president, ending more than 680 years of foreign monarchy. Danish King Christian X sends a message of congratulations.

events.title: Wartime boom transforms economy
events.description: By the end of WWII, Iceland has gone from one of Europe's poorest countries to one of the world's wealthiest, having amassed currency reserves and eliminated unemployment.

events.title: US ends formal defence role
events.description: Iceland and the United States agree to end US responsibility for Iceland's defence, though the US retains rights to re-establish a military presence at Keflavik should war threaten.

events.title: Marshall Aid - highest per capita
events.description: Iceland receives the most Marshall Aid per capita of any European country at US$209, more than double the Netherlands. Funds finance a modernised fishing fleet and processing infrastructure.

events.title: Iceland founds NATO
events.description: Iceland becomes a charter member of NATO, with the reservation that it will never take part in offensive action. The accession is marked by an anti-NATO riot in Reykjavik.

events.title: US-Iceland Defence Agreement
events.description: Following the outbreak of the Korean War, Iceland and the United States sign the bilateral Defence Agreement. US forces return to Keflavik, where they will remain for 55 years.

events.title: Proto Cod War
events.description: Iceland extends its fishery limits from 3 to 4 nautical miles, triggering the first of four militarised disputes with the United Kingdom over North Atlantic fishing rights.

events.title: First Cod War
events.description: Iceland extends fishery limits to 12 nautical miles. Icelandic patrol ships and British trawlers clash; the Royal Navy is deployed to the contested waters.

events.title: Second Cod War
events.description: Iceland extends fishery limits to 50 nautical miles. Repeated clashes with British trawlers and Royal Navy frigates ensue, again resolved by Icelandic threats to leave NATO.

events.title: Third Cod War
events.description: Iceland extends limits to 200 nautical miles, breaks off diplomatic relations with the UK, and threatens to close the Keflavik base. NATO pressure forces UK concessions and Iceland prevails.

events.title: Vigdis Finnbogadottir elected president
events.description: Vigdis Finnbogadottir is sworn in as President of Iceland, becoming the world's first directly elected female head of state. She will serve for 16 years.

events.title: Reagan-Gorbachev Reykjavik Summit
events.description: US President Ronald Reagan and Soviet General Secretary Mikhail Gorbachev meet at Hofdi House in Reykjavik. The summit collapses over SDI but paves the way for the INF Treaty a year later.

events.title: David Oddsson begins market reforms
events.description: The Independence Party under David Oddsson forms a coalition with the Social Democrats and begins privatising state-owned companies and liberalising the Icelandic economy.

events.title: Iceland joins the EEA
events.description: Iceland enters the European Economic Area, gaining access to the EU single market without joining the bloc. The reform deepens economic integration with Europe.

events.title: Independence-Progressive coalition
events.description: The Independence Party forms a long-running coalition with the Progressive Party. Privatisation continues; corporate income tax is cut from around 50 percent to 18 percent.

events.title: Iraq War - Coalition of the Willing
events.description: Iceland signs up as a nominal member of the Coalition of the Willing supporting the US-led invasion of Iraq, a domestically contentious decision.

events.title: Oddsson steps down after 13 years
events.description: David Oddsson steps down as prime minister after 13 years, the longest tenure of any Icelandic PM. He is succeeded by Progressive Party leader Halldor Asgrimsson.

events.title: US closes Keflavik Air Base
events.description: The United States withdraws the last of its forces from Keflavik, ending a continuous military presence dating to 1951. The 1951 Defence Agreement remains in force as the legal basis for cooperation.

events.title: Banking system collapses
events.description: Iceland's three major banks - Glitnir, Landsbanki and Kaupthing - fail within days of each other. Iceland seeks emergency loans from the IMF and friendly states; the krona collapses.

events.title: Pots and Pans Revolution
events.description: Sustained protests outside the Althing force the resignation of Geir Haarde's government - the first European government brought down by the financial crisis.

events.title: Sigurdardottir - first openly gay PM
events.description: Social Democrat Johanna Sigurdardottir becomes prime minister, the world's first openly homosexual head of government of the modern era, leading a Social Democrat-Left Green coalition.

events.title: Icesave dispute
events.description: The UK and Netherlands demand Iceland repay British and Dutch depositors who lost savings in the failed Icesave online bank. Icelandic voters reject repayment in two referendums.

events.title: Net emigration of 5,000
events.description: The financial crisis triggers the greatest net emigration from Iceland since 1887, with 5,000 more people leaving than arriving in a country of just over 300,000.

events.title: Eyjafjallajokull grounds European flights
events.description: An eruption at Eyjafjallajokull sends a 9km ash plume across Western Europe, shutting most of European airspace for nearly a month - the largest air-travel disruption since WWII.

events.title: Economy returns to growth
events.description: Iceland's GDP grows 1.6 percent in 2012, marking the formal end of the post-crisis recession. Capital controls remain in place.

events.title: Centre-right returns under Gunnlaugsson
events.description: Voters reject continued austerity. The Independence and Progressive parties return to government in coalition, with Sigmundur David Gunnlaugsson as prime minister.

events.title: Gudni Th. Johannesson elected president
events.description: Historian Gudni Th. Johannesson takes office as President of Iceland after a snap election triggered by the Panama Papers scandal.

events.title: Jakobsdottir leads Left-Green coalition
events.description: Katrin Jakobsdottir of the Left-Green Movement becomes prime minister at the head of an unusual cross-spectrum coalition with the Independence and Progressive parties.

events.title: Tri-party coalition continues
events.description: After the parliamentary election, Jakobsdottir's tri-party coalition is renewed - an unusual continuation of a cross-ideological government in Western Europe.

events.title: Benediktsson succeeds Jakobsdottir
events.description: Bjarni Benediktsson of the Independence Party becomes prime minister after Katrin Jakobsdottir steps down to contest the presidential election.

events.title: Halla Tomasdottir wins presidency
events.description: Businesswoman Halla Tomasdottir wins Iceland's presidential election, becoming the country's second female head of state after Vigdis Finnbogadottir.

events.title: Frostadottir - Social Democrat PM
events.description: The centre-left Social Democratic Alliance becomes the largest party in a snap election. Kristrun Frostadottir becomes prime minister, ending the Independence-Progressive-Left Green coalition.
